name: Bedrock API and Model Invocation
description: Using the Amazon Bedrock InvokeModel and Converse APIs to programmatically interact with foundation models, including request formatting and response handling
key_facts: InvokeModel API accepts model-specific request payloads and returns model-specific responses
Converse API provides a unified interface across models with standardized request/response formats
Model IDs follow the format provider.model-name:version (e.g., anthropic.claude-3-sonnet-20240229-v1:0)
Request parameters include max_tokens, temperature, top_p, top_k, and stop_sequences
Bedrock supports cross-region inference for higher throughput and availability
common_misconceptions: All Bedrock models use the same request format (each model has its own payload structure unless using Converse API)
InvokeModel is synchronous only (InvokeModelWithResponseStream provides streaming responses)

name: Streaming Responses and Multi-Modal Models
description: Implementing streaming inference for real-time token-by-token output and working with models that accept multiple input modalities
key_facts: InvokeModelWithResponseStream returns tokens as they are generated for lower time-to-first-token
Streaming uses server-sent events (SSE) or chunked transfer encoding
Multi-modal models accept text, images, and documents as input in a single request
Images are passed as base64-encoded strings or S3 URIs in the request payload
The Converse API simplifies multi-modal input across different model providers
common_misconceptions: Streaming produces different outputs than non-streaming (the final output is identical)
All Bedrock models support multi-modal input (only specific models like Claude 3 and Titan support images)

name: Embeddings and Vector Search
description: Generating vector embeddings from text and using vector databases for semantic similarity search in RAG and recommendation systems
key_facts: Embeddings convert text into dense numerical vectors that capture semantic meaning
Amazon Titan Embeddings and Cohere Embed models are available on Bedrock for embedding generation
Vector databases (OpenSearch Serverless, Aurora PostgreSQL with pgvector, Pinecone) store and search embeddings
Cosine similarity and L2 distance are common metrics for finding similar embeddings
Embedding dimensions affect accuracy and storage: higher dimensions capture more nuance but cost more
common_misconceptions: Embeddings preserve the original text (they are numerical representations that cannot be reversed to exact text)
Any embedding model works with any vector database equally well (dimension compatibility and normalization matter)

name: RAG with Bedrock Knowledge Bases
description: Implementing Retrieval Augmented Generation using Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases to ground model responses in enterprise data
key_facts: Knowledge Bases automatically ingest, chunk, embed, and index documents from S3 data sources
Supports multiple chunking strategies: fixed-size, semantic, and hierarchical chunking
RetrieveAndGenerate API combines retrieval and generation in a single call
Retrieve API returns source chunks with relevance scores for custom RAG pipelines
Supports document formats including PDF, TXT, HTML, Markdown, CSV, DOC, and XLS
common_misconceptions: Knowledge Bases require manual embedding generation (the service handles embedding automatically)
RAG eliminates all hallucinations (it reduces them but the model can still generate inaccurate information)

name: Bedrock Agents and Action Groups
description: Building autonomous AI agents that can plan multi-step tasks, invoke external APIs, and execute business workflows using Amazon Bedrock Agents
key_facts: Agents use foundation models to break down user requests into a sequence of steps
Action groups define API operations the agent can invoke, specified via OpenAPI schemas
Agents can query Knowledge Bases for information and call Lambda functions for custom logic
The agent orchestrates between reasoning, retrieving information, and taking actions
Session attributes maintain state across turns in multi-turn agent conversations
common_misconceptions: Agents execute code directly (they invoke pre-defined Lambda functions through action groups)
Agents always follow a fixed workflow (they dynamically plan and adjust steps based on the model's reasoning)

name: Guardrails Implementation
description: Implementing and configuring Amazon Bedrock Guardrails for content filtering, topic blocking, PII handling, and contextual grounding checks
key_facts: Guardrails can be applied via the ApplyGuardrail API or integrated directly into Converse/InvokeModel calls
Content filters have configurable strength levels (NONE, LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH) for categories like hate, violence, and sexual content
Denied topic policies use natural language descriptions to block specific conversation topics
PII filters can BLOCK or ANONYMIZE sensitive data types like SSN, email, phone number, and credit card
Contextual grounding checks detect hallucination by comparing responses against provided source material
common_misconceptions: Guardrails only filter model outputs (they can filter both user inputs and model outputs)
Guardrails are model-specific (they work across all Bedrock models and even external models via ApplyGuardrail API)

name: LangChain and Orchestration Frameworks
description: Using LangChain and other orchestration frameworks to build complex generative AI applications with chains, memory, and tool integration
key_facts: LangChain provides abstractions for chains, agents, memory, and retrieval integration
LangChain's Bedrock integration allows using Bedrock models as LLM providers in chains
Chains compose multiple steps: retrieval, prompt formatting, model invocation, and output parsing
Memory modules maintain conversation history across interactions (buffer, summary, vector store backed)
LangChain agents can use tools defined as Python functions or API wrappers
common_misconceptions: LangChain is required to use Bedrock (Bedrock has its own native APIs and agents)
LangChain handles deployment and scaling (it is an orchestration library; deployment requires separate infrastructure)

name: Content Filtering and Safety Controls
description: Implementing multi-layered content safety including toxicity detection, topic restrictions, and word filters to prevent harmful AI outputs
key_facts: Content filters evaluate text across categories: hate, insults, sexual, violence, misconduct, and prompt attacks
Word filters block specific words, phrases, or regex patterns in both inputs and outputs
Managed word lists provide pre-built profanity filters across multiple languages
Prompt attack detection identifies and blocks jailbreak and prompt injection attempts
Filter actions can block the request entirely or provide a canned response
common_misconceptions: Content filtering catches all harmful outputs (sophisticated adversarial prompts may still bypass filters)
Stricter filters are always better (overly strict filters cause false positives and degrade user experience)

name: PII Detection and Data Protection
description: Detecting and handling personally identifiable information in AI application inputs and outputs using automated redaction and masking
key_facts: Bedrock Guardrails PII filters detect 30+ PII types including SSN, credit card, email, and phone numbers
PII handling actions: BLOCK rejects the entire request, ANONYMIZE replaces PII with placeholder tags
Amazon Comprehend also provides PII detection and redaction as a standalone NLP service
Regex-based sensitive information filters can detect custom patterns like internal IDs or account numbers
PII filtering should be applied to both user inputs and model outputs
common_misconceptions: PII detection catches all sensitive data formats globally (it works best with standard formats and may miss region-specific patterns)
Anonymized data can be de-anonymized from the model's response (properly anonymized PII is replaced with generic tags)

name: Red Teaming and Model Evaluation
description: Systematically testing AI applications for vulnerabilities, biases, and failure modes through adversarial testing and automated evaluation
key_facts: Red teaming probes AI systems for harmful outputs, jailbreaks, prompt injections, and policy violations
Amazon Bedrock Model Evaluation supports automatic evaluation using built-in and custom metrics
Evaluation metrics include accuracy, robustness, toxicity, and task-specific performance measures
Human evaluation workflows complement automated metrics for subjective quality assessment
Regular red teaming should be performed after model updates, prompt changes, and guardrail modifications
common_misconceptions: Red teaming is a one-time pre-launch activity (it should be continuous throughout the application lifecycle)
Automated evaluation replaces human red teaming (both are needed for comprehensive safety assessment)

name: Responsible AI Metrics and Governance
description: Measuring and monitoring AI application safety, fairness, and quality using quantitative metrics and governance frameworks
key_facts: ROUGE and BLEU scores measure text quality by comparing model output to reference text
BERTScore uses embeddings to measure semantic similarity between generated and reference text
Toxicity scores measure the probability of harmful content in model outputs
Groundedness metrics evaluate whether responses are supported by retrieved context
CloudWatch metrics and alarms can monitor guardrail intervention rates in production
common_misconceptions: High ROUGE scores always indicate good quality (they measure overlap, not factual correctness or coherence)
A single metric is sufficient to evaluate a generative AI application (multiple metrics across safety, quality, and relevance are needed)

name: Token Optimization and Cost Management
description: Strategies for minimizing token usage and managing costs in generative AI applications, including prompt compression and model selection
key_facts: Bedrock pricing is per input and output token; reducing token count directly reduces cost
Prompt optimization techniques include removing unnecessary context, using concise instructions, and limiting output length
Smaller models (e.g., Haiku vs Sonnet) offer lower per-token costs for simpler tasks
Provisioned Throughput provides consistent performance at a fixed hourly rate for high-volume workloads
Batch inference processes large datasets at up to 50% lower cost than on-demand pricing
common_misconceptions: Longer prompts always produce better results (concise, well-structured prompts often outperform verbose ones)
Provisioned Throughput is always more cost-effective (it only saves money above a certain usage threshold)

name: Caching, Batching, and Performance Optimization
description: Implementing caching strategies and batch processing to improve performance and reduce costs in production generative AI applications
key_facts: Prompt caching reduces cost and latency for repeated prompt prefixes (system prompts, few-shot examples)
Semantic caching stores responses for semantically similar queries using embedding-based matching
Bedrock batch inference processes jobs asynchronously from S3 input files at reduced cost
ElastiCache or DynamoDB can cache frequent query-response pairs to avoid redundant model invocations
Response streaming reduces perceived latency by showing tokens as they are generated
common_misconceptions: Caching works well for all generative AI use cases (highly personalized or creative tasks have low cache hit rates)
Batch inference provides real-time results (it is asynchronous and designed for offline processing)

name: Latency Optimization
description: Techniques for reducing end-to-end latency in generative AI applications including model selection, architecture patterns, and infrastructure optimization
key_facts: Time-to-first-token (TTFT) and tokens-per-second (TPS) are key latency metrics for generative AI
Smaller models have lower latency; use model routing to direct simple queries to faster models
Provisioned Throughput eliminates cold start and provides consistent low-latency responses
Reducing input prompt length directly reduces processing time and TTFT
Cross-region inference distributes load and can reduce latency for global applications
common_misconceptions: Latency depends only on model size (network latency, prompt length, and output length all contribute significantly)
Provisioned Throughput always provides lower latency than on-demand (it provides consistent latency, not necessarily lower latency)

name: Prompt Testing and Regression Testing
description: Systematically testing prompt effectiveness, validating model behavior, and preventing regressions when prompts or models change
key_facts: Prompt test suites contain input-output pairs that define expected behavior for critical scenarios
A/B testing compares different prompts or models on live traffic to measure performance differences
Regression testing ensures prompt changes do not degrade performance on previously passing test cases
Bedrock Prompt Management stores and versions prompts for reproducibility and rollback
Evaluation datasets should cover edge cases, adversarial inputs, and representative production queries
common_misconceptions: Prompt testing only needs to happen during initial development (prompts should be re-tested when models are updated or new edge cases are discovered)
Manual spot-checking is sufficient for prompt validation (systematic automated testing with metrics is needed for production reliability)
